DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-19-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT ALAN ZIMMERMAN, MONICA ZIMMERMAN's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Alan Zimmerman and Monica Zimmerman's demurrer to plaintiff Scott Batchelor's second amended complaint ("SAC") is overruled.

In March 2022, the parties executed a pre-suit employment settlement agreement. (SAC Ex. 1.) Defendants would "pay Batchelor the total gross sum of [$85,500] less withholdings and deductions." (Id. provision 3(a).) $36,200 went to attorney's fees. (Id. provision 3(a)(1).) The remaining $49,300 was payable to Batchelor "and shall be reported by the COMPANY on an IRS Form W-2." (Id. provision 3(a)(2).) Batchelor sued for breach of contract, alleging defendants breached by taking the entire settlement out as W2 wages instead of deducting attorneys' fees off the top. Defendants demur on multiple grounds.

First, defendants argue the plain language of the settlement agreement, which is attached to the SAC as an exhibit, shows they complied. On a demurrer to a breach of contract claim, the test is "whether the alleged agreement is 'reasonably susceptible' to the meaning ascribed to it in the complaint [citation]." (Klein v. Chevron U.S.A., Inc. (2012) 202 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1384-1385.) Batchelor's interpretation that attorneys' fees were not subject to W-2 withholdings is reasonable at this stage, and he has adequately pled breach of contract.

Second, defendants argue the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction because only the United States Tax Court can interpret federal tax law per 26 U.S.C. 7442. This breach of contract action does not require interpretation or application of federal tax law and thus section 7442 is inapposite.

Third, the existence of a covenant not to sue is not grounds for sustaining this demurrer. Batchelor's position that defendants breached allowing him to maintain the action, is reasonable.

Fourth, the court declines to "bar Plaintiff from any recover" at the pleadings stage because he allegedly breached confidentiality. (Mot. 12:6-7.)

Fifth, "[d]emurrers for uncertainty are disfavored and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond." (Morris v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2022) 78 Cal.App.5th 279, 292.) Here, Batchelor attaches the agreement and identifies how defendants breached. These allegations are sufficiently detailed, and defendants can reasonably respond.

Sixth, Batchelor has legal capacity to sue to enforce a contract to which he is a party.

Seventh, the Commissioner of the Internal Revenue Service is not required to be joined in this contract action.

Eighth, a demurrer is not the appropriate vehicle by which to dispose of an improper remedy like recission. (See Caliber Bodyworks, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 365, 385.)

Defendants' requests for judicial notice are granted. Responsive pleadings are now due by April 19, 2024.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-19-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT ALAN ZIMMERMAN, MONICA ZIMMERMAN's MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Alan Zimmerman and Monica Zimmerman's motion to strike portions of plaintiff Scott Batchelor's second amended complaint ("SAC") is denied.

The "use of the motion to strike should be cautious and sparing," not "a procedural 'line item veto' for the civil defendant." (PH II, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1680, 1983.) Here, defendants move to strike the entire second amended complaint, each cause of action within, the entire prayer for relief, and over thirty more portions of the complaint. Defendants largely repeat arguments laid out in their companion demurrer. These arguments test the truth of Batchelor's allegations and are not grounds for granting the motion.

Responsive pleadings are now due by April 19, 2024.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Motion To File First Amended Complaint Under Seal; Memorandum Of Points And Authorites; Declaration Of Janine R. Menhennet
Off Calendar Sep-29-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Janine Menhennet, Esq. 858-583-6972 Kased Law Group for Plaintiff.
Douglas MacLellan, Esq. 415-447-8300 Law Office of Kaplan & MacLellan for Defendant.

ARGUED.The Court shares its views on CRC 2.550 and 2.551. Ms.Menhennet informs the Court they did comply with the courtesy copy rule. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF SCOTT BATCHELOR'S Motion To File First Amended Complaint Under Seal; Memorandum Of Points And Authorites; Declaration Of Janine R. Menhennet

Denied without prejudice. (1) The court does not have courtesy copies of the moving papers. Compare Local Rule 2.7B. (2) The memorandum says copies of the "redacted and unredacted" papers have been "filed" with the court, but the court's file does not show those. (3) The motion apparently seeks sealing of the entire first amended complaint, which is plainly overbroad. (4) Finally, the parties are reminded that while an agreement between parties may require a party to lodge materials provisionally under seal, an order actually sealing a document, or a word or phrase in it, is not controlled by the parties' agreement but may be granted only if the strict requirements of CRC 2.550 ff. are met. Proposed orders must conform to the requirements of CRC 2.550(d) and 2.551(e), and the facts reflected in such orders must be supplied by admissible evidence in e.g. declarations.

A new motion may be filed a for hearing before Judge Curtis Karnow in Dept. 611 after consulting with his clerk for a hearing date and time.

The Court prepares an order. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Curtis Karnow, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/CK)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Jun-09-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JANINE R. MENHENNET, Esq. of Kased Law Group APC (Tel: 619-333-6373) for plaintiff;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS ALAN ZIMMERMAN, AND MONICA ZIMMERMAN'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS AND COMPLAINT.

Matter discussed. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Specially-appearing defendants Alan P. Zimmerman and Monica K. Zimmerman, individually and doing business as Mark Adam & Co.'s motion to quash service of summons and original complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 418.10, which is unopposed, is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE IN PART. The court quashes Plaintiff Scott Batchelor's service of summons and service of his original complaint. (See Code Civ. Proc., 418.10, subd. (a)(1).) Specially-appearing Defendants' request that the court "quash" its April 21, 2023 order granting leave to file the original complaint under seal is denied without prejudice. Section 418.10 authorizes orders to quash service of certain documents; it does not authorize orders striking, voiding or quashing orders of the court. Put another way, section 418.10 is a vehicle for addressing certain issues regarding personal jurisdiction. Personal jurisdiction over named defendants was irrelevant to the issues presented in Plaintiff's sealing motion. If Specially-appearing Defendants still desire relief from the April 2023 order, they are free to seek such relief by appropriate application or motion. Notably, though, the April 2023 order was addressed only to the original complaint, which has now been superseded by an amended complaint, and, to date, the court has not authorized the sealing of any information in the amended complaint.

Order signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
